name: Aldo Moro Death
description: The assassination of Aldo Moro, former Italian Prime Minister, in 1978 by the Red Brigades (Brigate Rosse) is a pivotal event in Italian history that continues to fuel numerous conspiracy theories. While the official narrative attributes Moro's kidnapping and murder solely to the Red Brigades, various alternative theories suggest involvement from other actors, including right-wing extremist groups, elements within the Italian state (SISMI), the CIA, the KGB, and even the Mafia. These theories often allege that Moro's attempts to establish a 'historic compromise' (compromesso storico) between the Christian Democrats (DC) and the Italian Communist Party (PCI) were seen as a threat to the established order, both nationally and internationally, leading to his removal. Proponents point to inconsistencies in the official investigation, alleged collaboration between the Red Brigades and other groups, and the presence of 'unidentified' figures during the kidnapping and imprisonment as evidence. Mainstream historians and investigators largely dismiss these theories, citing a lack of concrete evidence and attributing the inconsistencies to the chaotic nature of the investigation and the conflicting testimonies of former Red Brigades members. They argue that the Red Brigades, driven by their own ideological motivations, were solely responsible for Moro's death, and that the conspiracy theories serve to obfuscate the group's internal dynamics and objectives. The event remains a deeply sensitive and controversial topic in Italy, influencing political discourse and historical interpretations.
subtitle: Political assassination and conspiracy theories.

claims.claim: The Red Brigades acted alone in Moro's kidnapping and murder.
claims.evidence: Numerous BR members confessed and provided details of the operation, claiming full responsibility.
claims.criticism: Confessions are inconsistent, potentially coerced, and don't explain all aspects of the case. Some BR members claim outside involvement.

claims.claim: Foreign intelligence agencies (CIA, KGB) were involved in Moro's death to prevent the 'historic compromise'.
claims.evidence: Allegations of CIA/KGB agents being present during the kidnapping and providing support/manipulation to the Red Brigades. Declassified documents with suspicious redactions.
claims.criticism: No conclusive evidence links foreign agencies directly to the event. The 'historic compromise' was primarily an Italian political matter. Redactions are common in declassified documents for national security reasons.

claims.claim: Right-wing extremist groups collaborated with the Red Brigades to eliminate Moro.
claims.evidence: Connections between some Red Brigades members and right-wing groups. The presence of 'unidentified' individuals at the kidnapping scene.
claims.criticism: These connections are tenuous and don't prove direct involvement in Moro's assassination. 'Unidentified individuals' are often attributed to mistaken identities or misremembered details in a chaotic situation.

keyFigures.name: Steve Pieczenik
keyFigures.role: US Crisis Management Expert involved in the crisis

keyFigures.name: Miguel Gotor
keyFigures.role: Historian, author of critical analyses of the conspiracy theories

mythologyConnections: The sacrifice of a leader for the sake of a greater cause (analogous to classical myths).
The theme of betrayal and conspiracy (common motif in mythology)

timeline.event: Aldo Moro kidnapped by the Red Brigades
Aldo Moro's body found in the trunk of a Renault 4 in Rome
Emergence and proliferation of various conspiracy theories surrounding Moro's death
Parliamentary commissions and investigations continue to examine the events surrounding Moro's death
